fi -10-041(anouol
This letter is SUPERSEDED by the 1998 amendments to
Regulation 18530. Regulation 18530 revised the Commission's
interpretation of Government Code Section 85300. Prior to the
amendment, the Commission interpreted Section 85300 to prohibit the
use of public resources for campaign purposes. Amended Regulation
18530 provides that Section 85300 only prohibits the payment of public
moneys for the public financing of elections.


CalifOrnia
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
February 20′  ■990
」ohn R. Calhoun
City AttOrney
city of Long Beach
City Hall
333 WeSt Ocean Boulevard
Long Beach′  CA  90802
SUPERSEDED BY 1998
AⅣlENDⅣIENTS T0
REGULAT10N 18530
Re:  Your Request for AdV■
Ce
Our File No. A-90-047
Dear Mr. calhoun:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behatf of ttayor Ernie KeIl regarding his responsibilities Pursuant
to the provi-sions of the Politica1 Reforn Act (the trActtr) 'rl
your letter seeks follow-up advice regarding our letter to Sheila
M. P".ro.,=, Executive Assiitant to the Mayor and City Council of
Long Beach. (Parsons Advice Letter, No. A-89-254, copy enclosed')
OUESTIONS
1. Is the production at city expense of an informational
cable television- program whj'ch j-s hosted by the mayor a prohibited
mass mailing?
2. Is the use of public funds to pay for a public access
cable television prograln hosted by the mayor an expenditure of
p"rii"-i""as 
for the-purpose of seeking erective office?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Distribution of a proqran over the airwaves is not the
distribution of a tangible itert. 
Consequently, televised programs
are not mass rnailings under the Act'
2. 
The use of public funds in the production-of the cable
television program holted by the mayor does not vioLate secElon
ll 
covernment code sections 81ooo-91015' A11 statutory refer-
ences are to the Government code unless otherwise -ildic?!:g:- -com-
;;;;i";-;d,rl.tior,. 
"ppE.t at 2 california code of Resutations
Section 18ooo, "t t""i- -efl-ieierences -to regulations are to Title
;;-;i;i=i"" 
o' o?EE-c.rifornia code of RegTurations'
428 J Street,Suite 800 ● P.0.Box 807 0 SacramentO CA 95804・0807 0 (916)322´5660
′
ヽ


File No. A-90-047
Page 2
85300 if those funds are not used to advocate or promote his elec-
tion to office. 
However, this advice applies only to the provi-
sions of the Act. ottrer restrictions nay be inposed by la$rs that
are not set forth in the Act and are, therefore, outside the
Commission's jurisdiction.
FACTS
The city of Long Beach is governed by a mayor and an 8-member
city council. The mayor is elected in a citywide election. 
The
councilrnembers are elected by district. 
The nayoral election will
occur this year.
The city of Long Beach produces a television program to
publicize current issues and city policy. 
The program is hosted
by the rnayor and produced for the cable television public access
channel. It is funded by public access funds and general fund
rooney that is received by the city fron franchise fees. However,
you have recently become concerned over allegations that use of
city funds to produce the program is a misuse of public.money in
violation of section 85300 of the Act.
ANALYS I S
The Appropriate Use of Public Funds
As noted in our previous letter to your city (Parsons Advice
Letter, supra), the coBmission cannot deteruine whether the
expenditures approved by the Long Beach city council are
legitimate government expenditures. (@, e.q. Penal code Section
54 Ca1. App. 3d 640; and People v. Battin (L978) 77 CaI. App. 3d
635.) We can only advise as to whether such expenditures violate
the provisions of the Political ReforE Act'
other provisions of the lal which nay aPPly to these facts,
such as penal code section 424, which deals vith the rnisuse of
public moneys, .." ""i=ia" ttre' juiisaiction of the coDmission.2l
ihus, nothing in this letter should be construed to be an
authorization for any specific expenditures made by a state or
local government agency.
l{ass l.{ailinds
In June 1988 Proposition 73 aDended Section 89001 of the Act'
This section now provides: rtNo ner,sletter or other mass nailing
shall be sent at i:ublic expense.rt Holrever, a literal 
reading of
this section led to a conclusion that a1l mass rnailings involving
p"uii. funds, irrespective of content or purpose, were prohibited'
2/ Penal code section 424
district attorneY and the
is interpreted and enforced bY the
Attorney General.


riLe No. A-9o-047
Page 3
In response to a variety of questions concerning the distribution
of tax notices, tax refund checks, conmunity colLege schedules,
sample ballots, and other rnass uailings customarily sent by -
govLrnment agencies, the conmission aiopted negulalion fAg6L3/ to
cl,arify which nailings !/ere pernissible and which were prohibited
under fhe Act. a/
Reqnrlation 18901 provides that a mass roailing is prohibited
only if all the following app1ys
(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any
Deans, to the recipient at his or her
residence, place of emplolment or business, or
post office box. For purposes of this
subdivision (a) (1), the iten delivered to the
recipient must be a tanoible iten, such as a
videotape, record, or button. or a nritten
docunent.
(2) The iten sent either:
(A) Features an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces
or sends the naj.ling, or
(B) Includes the name, office,
photograph, or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated lrith the
agency which produces or sends the mail-
ing, and is prepared or sent in co-
operation, consultation, coordinati.on, or
concert lrith the elected officer;
(3) (A) jlny of the costs of distribution
is paid for with public noneys i or
(B)
Costs of design′  production′
and printing exceeding 95o.oo are paid
with public moneys, and the design,
product j,on, or printing is done with the
3/ Regulation 18901 was amended by the Conmission in December
1989. The regulation will be subnitted to the Office of
Adninistrative Law in the near future. Effective January 18,
1990, the Conmission began advising on the basis of the language
of the new regulation. The issues in this letter have been
analyzed under the amended regulation. A copy of the anended
regutation is enclosed.
4/ The commission's authority to interpret section 89001 to avoid
the absurd results of its literal application was upheld in watson
v. Fair Political Practices Con., case No. 8042250, second Dist'
Court of Appeal, 90 D.A.R. 1312 (february 6, 1990).


Fl■e No. A-90-047
Page 4
intent of sending the iteru other than as
permitted by this regrulation.
(4) More than two hundred substantially
sinilar itens are sent, in a single calendar
nonth, excluding any iten sent in response to
an unsolicited request and any iten described
in subdivision (b);
(Emphasis added. )
Thus, under Regulation 18901, the nayor's Progran could only
be a prohibited mass nailing where it is delivered as a tangible
iten to the recipient at his or her residence, Place of enployment
or business, or post office box. Since distribution over the
aj.rwaves is not the distribution of a tangible itern, televised
prograns are not mass nailings under the Act. Consequently, we
confirur the conclusion in the Parsons Advice Letter that the
progran is not a rrnass nailing sent at public expenserr within the
neaning of section 89001, even though public moneys are used to
produce it.
Expenditures for the Purpose of seekincr Elective office
In your request for advice you specifically asked whether
section 85300 restricts the production of the program. Section
85300 provides:
No public officer shall expend and no
candidate shall accept any public noneys for the
purpose of seeking elective office.
rrPublic moneysrr is defined to incLude all bonds and evidence
of indebtedness, and all moneys belonging to the state, or any
city, county, tolrn, district, 
or public agency there j-n, and all
moneys, bonds, and evidence of indebtedness received or held by.
state, county, district, 
city, totrn, or public agency officers in
their official capacity. (Section 85102(e); Penal code section
426.)
consequently, all Doneys held by the City of Long Beach are
public money and, according to Section 85300 of the Act, may not
Le used for the purpose of seek5.ng elective office. 
Prior to the
enactment of Proposition 73, the Commission was never confronted
directly with the issue of when payments are for rrthe purPose of
seeking- elective officel r The Connission has, however, dealt with
issues concerning disclosure of caropaign expenditures. under the
Act, a disclosable canpaign expenditure is defined as a rrpayment
made for political 
PurPoses. rr (ReguLation L8225, copy enclosed.)
Prior to the enactment of Proposition 73, the Commission
adopted Regrulation L842o I which addresses the question of whether
palients by a governnent agency night be.canpaign expenditures'
lf-a payrnent is deteruined to be a canpaign expenditure, the Act


File No. A-90-047
Page 5
requj,res the disclosure of the pa)rment by the beneficiary of the
payment or by the local government agency that made the pa)alent.
Regulation 1842o provides:
(a) Any candidate or connittee that receives
contributions from a state or loca1 government
agency shaII report receipt of those contributions.
(b) The pa)arent by a state or local govern-
ment agency of the salary or expenses of its
enployees or agents is an expenditure or contribu-
tion only if the salary or expenses are for
canpaign activities and meet the reguirements of 2
cal. Adn. Code Section 18423. For purposes of this
subsection, rrcampaign activitiestt shall include,
but are not linited to, the following:
(1) Arranging or coordinating a
campaign-reLated event;
(2) Acting in the capacity of the
campaign manager or coordinator;
(3) Soliciting, receiving or
acknowledging canpaign contributions or ar-
ranging for the raising of contributions,'
(4) Developing, writing or distributing
canpaign literature or naking arrangements for
carnpaign literature;
(5) Arranging for the deveLopment,
production or distribution of campaign
Iiterature;
(6) Preparing television, radio or
neqrspaper eanpaign advertiseuents,'
(7) Arranging for the develoPment,
production, publishing or broadcast of
canpaign advertisements I
(8) Establishing liaison with or co-
ordinating activities of campaign volunteersl 
.
(9) PreParing carnPaign budgets;
(10) Preparing caupaign statenents,' and
(11) Participating in partisan get out
the vote drives.
In light of this existing authority, we do not believe that
Section g55OO was intended to prohibit everv activitv that miqht


File No. A-9O-047
Page 6
indirectlv benefit an elected official's 
chances for reelection.
Arguably, all the activities of incuurbent el,ected officers in the
performance of their governmental duties indirectly benefit their
reelection. Instead, we conclude that sectj-on 853oo was intended
to prohibit the public financing of election campaigns,5/
consistent with this intent, where public moneys are spent to
advocate or promote a candidate's election to public office, Sec-
tion 85300 has been violated. 
we believe it is appropriate to
consider "campaign activitiestt as defined in Regulation 18420 as
being among the types of activities for which Section 85300
prohibits the expenditure of public moneys, This approach is
consistent with the approach taken by the courts concerning use of
public funds in election campaigns. The courts have distinguished
betlreen the appropriate use of public funds to give the pubic a
balanced informational presentation of issues surrounding an elec-
tion campaign, and the unla$rful use of public funds by a govern-
nent agency to take sides in an election canpaign. (Stanson v.
l.Iott, supra; Leaque of Women voters v. Countvrride Crin. Justice
coordination con. (1988) 2o3 Ca1. App. 3d 529.)
In viewing the sanrples of the program you have subnitted, we
do not believe that these programs fall within those activities
listed in Regulation 18420 or are otherwise within the prohibition
of section 85300. we note no references to the mayor's reeLection
or status as a candidate, nor to those of other public officials
appearing on the progran. Thus, the use of public funds in the
production of the programs does not violate Section 85300.
However, as we discussed above, please note that our advice ap-
pties only to the provisions of the Act. other areas of the Ia$,
that nay apply are. not within our jurisdiction.
If any further questions regarding this natter, please feel
free to contact ne at (915) 322-59oL.
Sincerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
General counsel
